name: View
summary: This class contains methods to manipulate the current point of view of the model. The drawing methods here (#draw_line, #draw_polyline, etc) are meant to be invoked within a tool's Tool#draw method. Calling them outside Tool#draw will have no effect. You access the View by calling the Model#active_view method.

methods.name: add_observer
methods.summary: The add_observer method is used to add an observer to the current object.
methods.remarks: The add_observer method is used to add an observer to the current object.

params.name: observer
params.doc: An observer.

returns.doc: true if successful, false if unsuccessful.

methods.name: average_refresh_time
methods.summary: The average_refresh_time is used to set the average time used to refresh the current model in the view.
methods.remarks: The average_refresh_time is used to set the average time used to refresh the current model in the view. This can be used to estimate the frame rate for an animation.

methods.name: center
methods.signature: center => Array(Integer, Integer) | center => Array(Float, Float)
methods.summary: The #center method is used to retrieve the coordinates of the center of the view in pixels.
methods.remarks: The #center method is used to retrieve the coordinates of the center of the view in pixels.

methods.name: corner
methods.signature: corner(index) => Array(Integer, Integer) | corner(index) => Array(Float, Float)
methods.summary: Note: If the view uses a Camera with a fixed aspect ratio, then the corners are the corners of the viewing are of the camera which might be different than the actual corners of the view itself.
methods.remarks: Note: If the view uses a Camera with a fixed aspect ratio, then the corners are the corners of the viewing are of the camera which might be different than the actual corners of the view itself. The #corner method is used to retrieve the coordinates of one of the corners of the view. The argument is an index between 0 and 3 that identifies which corner you want. This method returns an array with two integers which are the coordinates of the corner of the view in the view space. The indices are as follows: 0: CORNER_TOP_LEFT 1: CORNER_TOP_RIGHT 2: CORNER_BOTTOM_LEFT 3: CORNER_BOTTOM_RIGHT

params.name: index
params.doc: A value between (or including) 0 and 3 identifying the corner whose coordinate you want to retrieve.

returns.doc: a 2D array [x, y] representing the screen point in physical pixels.

methods.name: device_height
methods.summary: The #device_height method is used to retrieve the height of the viewport for the view in physical pixels.
methods.remarks: The #device_height method is used to retrieve the height of the viewport for the view in physical pixels.

returns.doc: the height of the viewport in physical pixels.

methods.name: device_width
methods.summary: The #device_width method is used to retrieve the width of the viewport for the view in physical pixels.
methods.remarks: The #device_width method is used to retrieve the width of the viewport for the view in physical pixels.

returns.doc: the width of the viewport in physical pixels.

methods.name: draw
methods.signature: draw(openglenum, points) => Sketchup::View | draw(openglenum, *points) => Sketchup::View | draw(openglenum, points, **options) => Sketchup::View | draw(openglenum, *points, **options) => Sketchup::View
methods.summary: Note: If you draw outside the model bounds you need to implement Tool#getExtents which returns a bounding box large enough to include the points you draw.
methods.remarks: Note: If you draw outside the model bounds you need to implement Tool#getExtents which returns a bounding box large enough to include the points you draw. Otherwise your drawing will be clipped. The #draw method is used to do basic drawing. This method can only be called from within the Tool#draw method of a tool that you implement in Ruby. The following constants are all OpenGL terms and have been externalized to Ruby. Here is a summary of their meanings: GL_POINTS Treats each vertex as a single point. Vertex n defines point n. N points are drawn. GL_LINES Treats each pair of vertices as an independent line segment. Vertices 2n-1 and 2n define line n. N/2 lines are drawn. GL_LINE_STRIP Draws a connected group of line segments from the first vertex to the last. Vertices n and n+1 define line n. N-1 lines are drawn. GL_LINE_LOOP Draws a connected group of line segments from the first vertex to the last, then back to the first. Vertices n and n+1 define line n. The last line, however, is defined by vertices N and 1. N lines are drawn. GL_TRIANGLES Treats each triplet of vertices as an independent triangle. Vertices 3n-2, 3n-1, and 3n define triangle n. N/3 triangles are drawn. GL_TRIANGLE_STRIP Draws a connected group of triangles. One triangle is defined for each vertex presented after the first two vertices. For odd n, vertices n, n+1, and n+2 define triangle n. For even n, vertices n+1, n, and n+2 define triangle n. N-2 triangles are drawn. GL_TRIANGLE_FAN Draws a connected group of triangles. One triangle is defined for each vertex presented after the first two vertices. Vertices 1, n+1, and n+2 define triangle n. N-2 triangles are drawn. GL_QUADS Treats each group of four vertices as an independent quadrilateral. Vertices 4n-3, 4n-2, 4n-1, and 4n define quadrilateral n. N/4 quadrilaterals are drawn. GL_QUAD_STRIP Draws a connected group of quadrilaterals. One quadrilateral is defined for each pair of vertices presented after the first pair. Vertices 2n-1, 2n, 2n+2, and 2n+1 define quadrilateral n. N/2-1 quadrilaterals are drawn. Note that the order in which vertices are used to construct a quadrilateral from strip data is different from that used with independent data. GL_POLYGON Draws a single, convex polygon. Vertices 1 through N define this polygon.

params.name: openglenum
params.doc: The item you are going to draw, one of the constants from the comments, such as GL_LINES.

methods.name: draw2d
methods.signature: draw2d(openglenum, points) => Sketchup::View | draw2d(openglenum, *points) => Sketchup::View | draw2d(openglenum, points, **options) => Sketchup::View | draw2d(openglenum, *points, **options) => Sketchup::View
methods.summary: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.
methods.remarks: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.0 this method accepted the points as physical screen coordinates. As of SketchUp 2025.0 the points are expected to be in logical screen coordinates. Older versions need to apply the scaling factor from UI.scale_factor to the points before passing them to this method. The #draw2d method is used to draw in screen space (using 2D screen coordinates) instead of 3D space. The second parameter is an Array of Geom::Point3d objects (or several individual Geom::Point3d objects). These Geom::Point3d objects are in screen space, not 3D space. The X value corresponds to the number of pixels from the left edge of the drawing area. The Y value corresponds to the number of pixels down from the top of the drawing area. The Z value is not used.

params.name: openglenum
params.doc: The item you are going to draw, one of the constants from the comments, such as GL_LINES.

methods.name: draw_lines
methods.summary: The draw_lines method is used to draw disconnected lines.
methods.remarks: The draw_lines method is used to draw disconnected lines. You must have an even number of points. This method is usually invoked within the draw method of a tool.

params.name: points
params.doc: An even number of Point3d objects.

methods.name: draw_lines
methods.summary: The draw_lines method is used to draw disconnected lines.
methods.remarks: The draw_lines method is used to draw disconnected lines. You must have an even number of points. This method is usually invoked within the draw method of a tool.

params.name: points
params.doc: An even number of Point3d objects.

methods.name: draw_points
methods.summary: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.
methods.remarks: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.0 this method accepted the size as physical pixels. As of SketchUp 2025.0 the points are expected to be in logical pixels. Older versions need to apply the scaling factor from UI.scale_factor to the size before passing them to this method. This method is used to draw points in model space.

params.name: points
params.doc: Model coordinates.

params.name: size
params.doc: Size of the point in pixels.

params.name: style
params.doc: 1 = open square 2 = filled square 3 = plus shape “+” 4 = cross shape “X” 5 = star shape “*” 6 = open triangle 7 = filled triangle

methods.name: draw_polyline
methods.summary: The draw_polyline method is used to draw a series of connected line segments from pt1 to pt2 to pt3, and so on.
methods.remarks: The draw_polyline method is used to draw a series of connected line segments from pt1 to pt2 to pt3, and so on. This method is usually invoked within the draw method of a tool.

params.name: points
params.doc: An even number of Point3d objects.

methods.name: draw_text
methods.signature: draw_text(point, text) => Sketchup::View | draw_text(point, text, options = {}) => Sketchup::View
methods.summary: Note: Under Windows the font name must be less than 32 characters - due to system limitations.
methods.remarks: Note: Under Windows the font name must be less than 32 characters - due to system limitations. Note: As of SU2017 this will automatically scale the font-size by the same factor as UI.scale_factor. Note: The font size is platform dependent. On Windows the method expects points, where on Mac it's pixels. See “Cross Platform Font Size” example for details. As of SketchUp 2025.0 you can use the :pixel_size or :point_size options to specify the size in pixels or points respectively. This method is used to draw text on the screen and is usually invoked within the draw method of a tool. The TextVerticalAlignCenter option will align the text to the center of the height of the first line, not the whole boundingbox of the text. To align around the full bounds of the text, use #text_bounds to compute the desired alignment. The vertical alignment can vary between fonts and platforms. It's recommended to test different fonts and find one that fits well across both platforms for your purposes. Example of different vertical alignment and text bounds:

params.name: point
params.doc: A Point3d object representing a 2D coordinate in view space.

params.name: text
params.doc: The text string to draw.

throws.when: if combining usage of :size, :pixel_size or :point_size options.

methods.name: graphics_engine
methods.summary: The #graphics_engine method is used query the type of the graphics engine that's currently used by this view.
methods.remarks: The #graphics_engine method is used query the type of the graphics engine that's currently used by this view.

returns.doc: Type of the graphics engine. :graphics_engine_classic or :graphics_engine_2024

methods.name: guess_target
methods.summary: The guess_target method is used to guess at what the user is looking at when you have a perspective view.
methods.remarks: The guess_target method is used to guess at what the user is looking at when you have a perspective view.

returns.doc: a Point3d object representing the point in the model that the user is likely interested in.

methods.name: inference_locked?
methods.summary: The inference_locked? method is used to determine if inference locking is on for the view.
methods.remarks: The inference_locked? method is used to determine if inference locking is on for the view.

methods.name: inputpoint
methods.signature: inputpoint(x, y) => Sketchup::InputPoint | inputpoint(x, y, inputpoint1) => Sketchup::InputPoint | inputpoint(x, y) => Sketchup::InputPoint | inputpoint(x, y, inputpoint1) => Sketchup::InputPoint
methods.summary: The #inputpoint method is used to retrieve an InputPoint.
methods.remarks: The #inputpoint method is used to retrieve an InputPoint. This will normally be used inside one of the mouse event handling methods in a tool. Usually, it is preferable to create the InputPoint first and then use the pick method on it.

params.name: x
params.doc: Screen coordinate in physical pixels.

params.name: y
params.doc: Screen coordinate in physical pixels.

methods.name: invalidate
methods.summary: Note: This is the preferred method to update the viewport.
methods.remarks: Note: This is the preferred method to update the viewport. Use this before trying to use #refresh. The invalidate method is used mark the view as in need of a redraw.

methods.name: last_refresh_time
methods.summary: The last_refresh_time method is used to retrieve the time for the last full view refresh.
methods.remarks: The last_refresh_time method is used to retrieve the time for the last full view refresh.

methods.name: load_texture
methods.summary: Note: Avoid loading and releasing textures within the Tool#draw event as that is not efficient.
methods.remarks: Note: Avoid loading and releasing textures within the Tool#draw event as that is not efficient. Note: SketchUp 2020.0-2022.0: To conserve resources on the user's machine, textures can be loaded only when there is a Ruby tool on the tool stack. Make sure to release the texture when it's no longer needed. Any textures not already released when the last Ruby tool on the tool stack is removed will be automatically released by SketchUp. As of SketchUp 2023.0 this automatic cleanup was removed to allow Overlays to draw textures. Loads a texture to be drawn with #draw or #draw2d.

returns.doc: A resource ID referring to the image loaded.

throws.when: if the provided ImageRep is not valid.
if a Ruby tool was not on the tool stack. (Applies to SketchUp 2020.0-2022.0).

methods.name: lock_inference
methods.signature: lock_inference => Sketchup::View | lock_inference(inputpoint) => Sketchup::View | lock_inference(inputpoint, inputpoint2) => Sketchup::View
methods.summary: The #lock_inference method is used to lock or unlock an inference.
methods.remarks: The #lock_inference method is used to lock or unlock an inference. This method will typically be called from inside a tool class when the user presses the shift key or arrow keys. With no arguments it unlocks all inferences. With one argument it locks inference based on that passed InputPoint's entities, e.g. along a Edge's line or a Face's plane. With two arguments, it locks inference along an axis.

methods.name: model
methods.summary: The model method is used to retrieve the model for the current view.
methods.remarks: The model method is used to retrieve the model for the current view.

methods.name: pick_helper
methods.signature: pick_helper => Sketchup::PickHelper | pick_helper(x, y, aperture = 0) => Sketchup::PickHelper | pick_helper(x, y, aperture = 0.0) => Sketchup::PickHelper
methods.summary: The #pick_helper method is used to retrieve a pick helper for the view.
methods.remarks: The #pick_helper method is used to retrieve a pick helper for the view.

params.name: x
params.doc: Screen coordinate in physical pixels.

params.name: y
params.doc: Screen coordinate in physical pixels.

params.name: aperture
params.doc: The size of the aperture in physical pixels.

methods.name: pickray
methods.signature: pickray(screen_point) => Array(Geom::Point3d, Geom::Vector3d) | pickray(x, y) => Array(Geom::Point3d, Geom::Vector3d) | pickray(screen_point) => Array(Geom::Point3d, Geom::Vector3d) | pickray(x, y) => Array(Geom::Point3d, Geom::Vector3d)
methods.summary: The #pickray method is used to retrieve a ray passing through a given screen position in the viewing direction.
methods.remarks: The #pickray method is used to retrieve a ray passing through a given screen position in the viewing direction.

params.name: screen_point
params.doc: Screen coordinates in physical pixels.

methods.name: pixels_to_model
methods.summary: Note: As of SU2017 this will automatically scale the pixel-size by the same factor as UI.
methods.remarks: Note: As of SU2017 this will automatically scale the pixel-size by the same factor as UI.scale_factor. The #pixels_to_model method is used to compute a model size from a pixel size at a given point. This method is useful for deciding how big to draw something based on a desired size in pixels.

params.name: pixels
params.doc: Logical pixels since SketchUp 2017.

params.name: point
params.doc: A model point where the size will be calculated from.

methods.name: refresh
methods.summary: Note: This method might impact performance and if used incorrectly cause instability or crashes.
methods.remarks: Note: This method might impact performance and if used incorrectly cause instability or crashes. Don't use this unless you have verified that you cannot use #invalidate instead. The refresh method is used to immediately force a redraw of the view.

methods.name: release_texture
methods.summary: Releases a texture loaded via #load_texture, freeing up it's memory.
methods.remarks: Releases a texture loaded via #load_texture, freeing up it's memory. It's good practice to do so whenever there is no longer any need for the resource. For example, when your tool deactivates you probably want to release your resources as you don't know if your tool will be used again.

returns.doc: true if texture was released. false otherwise.

methods.name: remove_observer
methods.summary: The remove_observer method is used to remove an observer from the current object.
methods.remarks: The remove_observer method is used to remove an observer from the current object.

params.name: observer
params.doc: An observer.

returns.doc: true if successful, false if unsuccessful.

methods.name: screen_coords
methods.summary: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.
methods.remarks: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.0 this method returned the points as physical screen coordinates. As of SketchUp 2025.0 the points are returned in logical screen coordinates. The #screen_coords method is used to retrieve the screen coordinates of the given point on the screen. The x and y values returned correspond to the x and y screen coordinates. Ignore the z values. If the referenced point is not in the current viewport, the x and/or y value may be negative.

params.name: model_point
params.doc: Model coordinate.

returns.doc: Screen coordinate in pixels (physical prior to SketchUp 2025.0, logical from 2025.0).

methods.name: set_color_from_line
methods.summary: Set the drawing color for the view based on the direction of a line that you want to draw.
methods.remarks: Set the drawing color for the view based on the direction of a line that you want to draw. These colors will match the axes colors in the SketchUp model (typically blue for straight up and down, etc.) This method is usually invoked within the draw method of a tool.

params.name: point1
params.doc: Point3d object representing first point in the line.

params.name: point2
params.doc: Point3d object representing the second point in the line.

methods.name: show_frame
methods.summary: The show_frame method is used to show a frame of an Animation object in the current view.
methods.remarks: The show_frame method is used to show a frame of an Animation object in the current view. You can supply an optional delay in seconds to wait before showing the next frame. This can be useful to control the speed at which the animation runs.

params.name: delay
params.doc: An optional delay in seconds.

methods.name: text_bounds
methods.summary: Note: Under Windows the font name must be less than 32 characters - due to system limitations.
methods.remarks: Note: Under Windows the font name must be less than 32 characters - due to system limitations. This method is used to compute the bounds of the text when using #draw_text. The bounds are not a tight fit around the top and bottom as they include varying amount of line spacing depending on the font used. The TextVerticalAlignCenter option will align the text to the center of the height of the first line, not the whole boundingbox of the text. Example of different vertical alignment and text bounds:

params.name: point
params.doc: A Point3d object representing a 2D coordinate in view space.

params.name: text
params.doc: The text string to draw.

params.name: options
params.doc: The text can be customized by providing a hash or named arguments of options.

throws.when: if combining usage of :size, :pixel_size or :point_size options.

methods.name: vpheight
methods.summary: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.
methods.remarks: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.0 this method returned the size as physical screen coordinates. As of SketchUp 2025.0 the size are returned in logical screen coordinates. The #vpheight method is used to retrieve the height of the viewport for the view.

returns.doc: the height of the viewport in physical pixels.

methods.name: vpwidth
methods.summary: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.
methods.remarks: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2025.0 this method returned the size as physical screen coordinates. As of SketchUp 2025.0 the size are returned in logical screen coordinates. The #vpwidth method is used to retrieve the width of the viewport for the view.

returns.doc: the width of the viewport in physical pixels.

methods.name: write_image
methods.signature: write_image(filename, width = view.vpwidth, height = view.vpheight, antialias = false, compression = 1.0) => Boolean | write_image(options) => Boolean | write_image(options) => Boolean
methods.summary: The #write_image method is used to write the current view to an image file.
methods.remarks: The #write_image method is used to write the current view to an image file. Supported file types are .png, .jpg, .jpeg, gif, .bmp, .tif. For other file formats available from the GUI in File > Export > 2D Graphics, .e.g .pdf, use Model#export.

params.name: filename
params.doc: The filename for the saved image

params.name: width
params.doc: Width in pixels, defaults to the current viewport width #vpwidth.

params.name: height
params.doc: Height in pixels, defaults to the current viewport height #vpheight.

params.name: compression
params.doc: Compression factor for JPEG images, between 0.0 and 1.0.

methods.name: zoom
methods.summary: The zoom method is used to zoom in or out by some zoom factor.
methods.remarks: The zoom method is used to zoom in or out by some zoom factor.

params.name: zoom_or_ents
params.doc: A Float zoom factor from 1.0 or larger or an Array or collection of entities to “zoom extents” around.

methods.name: zoom_extents
methods.summary: The zoom_extents method is used to zoom to the extents about the entire model, as if the user has selected the zoom extents command from the menu.
methods.remarks: The zoom_extents method is used to zoom to the extents about the entire model, as if the user has selected the zoom extents command from the menu.

properties.name: animation
properties.summary: The #animation= method is used to set an animation that is displayed for a view.
properties.remarks: The #animation= method is used to set an animation that is displayed for a view. See Animation for details on how to create an animation object.

properties.name: camera
properties.summary: The camera method is used to retrieve the camera for the view.
properties.remarks: The camera method is used to retrieve the camera for the view.

properties.name: drawing_color
properties.summary: The drawing_color method is used to set the color that is used for drawing to the view.
properties.remarks: The drawing_color method is used to set the color that is used for drawing to the view. This method is usually invoked within the draw method of a tool.

properties.name: dynamic
properties.remarks: Deprecated. This method is no longer doing anything. The dynamic= method allows you to degrade visual quality while improving performance when a model is large and view refresh time is slow. For example, if you were using a Ruby script to animate the camera through a large scene, you may want to set dynamic to true during that time. See also camera.rb which is part of the film and stage ruby scripts.

properties.name: field_of_view
properties.summary: The field_of_view method is used get the view's field of view setting, in degrees.
properties.remarks: The field_of_view method is used get the view's field of view setting, in degrees.

properties.name: line_stipple
properties.summary: The line_stipple= method is used to set the line pattern to use for drawing.
properties.remarks: The line_stipple= method is used to set the line pattern to use for drawing. The stipple pattern is given as a string. Valid strings are: This method is usually invoked within the draw method of a tool.

properties.name: line_width
properties.summary: Note: As of SU2017 this will automatically scale the line width by the same factor as UI.
properties.remarks: Note: As of SU2017 this will automatically scale the line width by the same factor as UI.scale_factor. Note: As of Sketchup 2026.0 positive values will be clamped to a minimum of 1.0. The line_width= method is used to set the line width to use for drawing. The value is a Double indicating the desired width in pixels. This method is usually invoked within the draw method of a tool.

properties.name: tooltip
properties.summary: Set a tooltip to display in the view.
properties.remarks: Set a tooltip to display in the view. This is useful for displaying tooltips in a tool that you write in Ruby.
